engineering.translation: Both paths reach the supreme destination. Sannyāsa-of-action and karma-yoga both arrive at identical liberation. But of these two, the yoga of action is more excellent (viśiṣyate) than the renunciation of action. Krishna's answer carries two structurally distinguishable halves that must not be flattened. At the level of destination, the two paths are equivalent — the engineer who has resigned versus the engineer who has continued can each be free of grasping and aversion; both reach the place. At the level of practice, the asymmetry is sharp — disciplined engagement is structurally easier than pure renunciation, because most apparent renunciation merely transports unrest in disguise. The reader who walks away with only 'both are equal' has lost half the verse; the reader who walks away with only 'shipping is better' has lost the other half. Both halves are load-bearing.
engineering.concrete_scenario: The senior engineer answers the IC's question. She does not give a single-pole answer. First half of the verdict: 'You may resign, sabbatical for twelve months, return as Postgres-migration contractor. You can stay, do the migration, wait for the architecture review. At the level of inner state — whether you are free of grasping at the next outcome — both paths can reach the same place.' Then, separately, the structural recommendation: 'But in practice, stay engaged. Most of the engineers I have watched quit-to-think have not stopped grasping; they have moved their grasping to a new setting. The discipline you are after is cultivated in the work, not by stepping out of it. Take the GitHub migration ticket this sprint. Read for the architecture review during your deep-work blocks. The Postgres-vector-DB decision is not improved by withdrawing from the merge queue.' Both halves are delivered. The IC is held to neither pole.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader hears only one half. 'Both paths are equal' alone produces relativism — the senior who shrugs at the IC's pressing question. 'Shipping is preferable' alone produces flat dogma — the senior who refuses to grant the validity of the renunciate path elsewhere. Krishna gives both halves because both are doctrinally load-bearing.
engineering.counter_example: When the engineer is in a literal physical or psychological crisis — burnout severe enough that continued shipping produces harm — the verse's preference for karma-yoga does not negate the need for actual rest. Krishna's preference is structural, not reckless. The senior teacher reading chapter 5 to an engineer in crisis must not apply the practical-asymmetry half without also applying the destination-equivalence half.
engineering.implication: When asked the engagement-vs-renunciation question, deliver both halves. Refuse to collapse the answer into a single pole. Most readers want one pole; the verse insists on two.
engineering.quotable_line: Both paths reach the same place; in practice, stay engaged. Two halves of the answer, not one.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 5.2 engineering layer drafted from triangulated source pack. Anchored to chapter-5 thesis: equivalence-of-paths-with-karma-yoga-preference, decoupled identity, sama-darśana, internal-bliss-source, brahma-nirvāṇa-as-operational-rung-with-honest-STRETCHED. Cross-references to chapters 2-4 preserved.
